subject: Support for lab contextual topic names.
commit_message:
Support for lab contextual topic names.

This work allows a drools application, with its drl
template to refer to the topic name by its invariable
canonical name, ie. POLICY-CL-MGT.    Since the drl
is a design time artifact, it is desired to know topics
by its canonical non-changeable name.

The actual per lab environment topic name may change
on a per deployment basis, for example POLICY-CL-MGT-WINDRIVER
or POLICY-CL-MGT-TLAB.   The template can still use
POLICY-CL-MGT without modification but the actual installation
configuration would use the "effectiveTopic" property to
point to the right topic on a per lab basis.

This also helps with installation (long story) since
the canonical topics will be known ahead of time.

Change-Id: I8322bf7e427569c37a76eea5ce6d5b9547cb2ff3
Issue-ID: POLICY-1534
Signed-off-by: Jorge Hernandez <jorge.hernandez-herrero@att.com>

Patch Set 1:

What would really be nice would be the ability to make the UEB/DMAAP decision using the effective topic. One way to do this in an upward-compatible manner would be to allow properties like 'source.topics' in addition to 'dmaap.source.topics', for example:

source.topics=DCAE-IN
...
source.topics.DCAE-IN.effectiveTopic=dmaap.com.att.dcae.DCAE-CL-EVENT

For topics defined generically like this, the 'effectiveTopic' must be defined, and the DMAAP/UEB type would need to be included, or somehow implied.

Patch Set 1:

Thanks Ralph. Jim?

Patch Set 1:

Why not just use a property name in the template, such as ${POLICY_CL_MGMT}?  Seems like it would address the concern cleanly, with minimal code impact.

Patch Set 1:

I think Ralph this is brought up as a side conversation not directly related to the review.  

This is my feedback: 

The ueb/dmaap consideration can be considered "almost" deprecated in practical terms.  

In ONAP for example, we use (which will fetch across the global set of endpoints for it):

PolicyEngine.manager.deliver(topic-name, Object-to-send).   

There's no distinction between UEB/DMAAP flag.   This is from the standpoint that it does not make any sense to talk to the same topic in a given lab deployment using ueb and dmaap, unless it is an obscure, bizarre lab configuration that somebody perhaps may want to support. 
   
The way I see this evolving when somebody has the cycles to do code cleanup is that one of ueb/dmaap should be integrated into one.  Actually the "dmaap" flag will try to use cambria libraries whenever possible.

I think effectively the 2 argument deliver(topic-name, object-to-send), and being able to override the "real" topic name with the optional effective topic, gets rid of the need to provision and expand topic names in the template, it is now a configuration problem instead, rather than being both a drl template and configuration problem.

Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1

(2 comments)

great! This is very helpful and will resolve issues I often run into in my test environment (e.g., "POLICY-CL-MGMT" hard-coded in templates)

Patch Set 1:

Jim, just lightly touching in your first comment, embedding ${X} information in the drl template as variables is pretty much what brmsgw does, and that approach I see it problematic at many levels, since drl in theory should be a design time artifact.  We can have conversation offline if you want to on this, but I think you're OK with the changes based on your last comment in the review.   As a note, taking out junits and reformatting the review is not sizable, around 100 lines.

Jorge: Yep, you explained your reasoning in your previous Post and it makes sense to me to separate this into config, as you said, rather than having it in templates, as well.  So only need to address my last review comments.

Patch Set 1:

> (1 comment)

still need additional test cases per the comment on line 83.

Regarding line 84, since you really want to verify that the reference is the same, that's fine.  However, in general, should use assertEquals() any time it doesn't matter if the reference is the same, as it provides more functionality in Eclipse if there's a mismatch.  (Eclipse provides a pop-up highlighting the differences.  That's why I sometimes use assertEquals(xxx.toString(), yyy.toString()) instead of assertEquals(xxx,yyy), because it highlights the mismatching fields.)
